That is the unique dedication
that an Indian girl inscribes upon
little volume of Indian sketches
that has just been published in Sc
attic, and is meeting already with
cordial reception.


Miss Florence Crane or Red
feather" as she prefers to be call
ed, is a graduate of the Tacoma
high school. Scarce past her goth
birthday she is a remarkably pref
ty and winning girl Long ago the
desire to write of her people game
to her to save for other genera
tons, red and white, the valorous
deeds and heroic tales of a race
that is fast passing away. Of her
self she writes:


AT an early age went my way
with brave heart through many
enemies and strange lands. At
length | came to the Flatheads OF
Montana, and there finding peace
and contentment, l wrote of the
tribe that received me so kindly.
| found my friends in Flathead land
walking the straight trail and
learned that in all of their history
they had not been guilty of the
blood of a white man."


Regarding the Indians Of the
northwest. Redfeather writes:


"The Siwashes of Puget Sound
always had an enduring faith, fear
ing punishment for evil doing, and
expecting reward for their bravery.
Among their gods was Mount
Rainier. This beautiful mountain,
